title: Arguing with an Auto Driver
situation: You're outside a metro station and need to get to Hauz Khas. The auto driver wants 200 rupees for a 50-rupee ride.

phrases.english: Brother, will you go to Hauz Khas?
phrases.context: 'bhaiya' (brother) is how you address auto/taxi drivers

phrases.hindi: arey, do sau? itna zyada kyun?
phrases.context: Show disbelief at the price — standard opening move
'na' at end = please/softener. Makes demands polite.

phrases.hindi: sau mein chalo, zyada nahi dunga
phrases.english: Go for 100, I won't give more
phrases.context: Final offer stance — firm but not rude

phrases.hindi: chodo, doosra auto dekh leta hoon
phrases.english: Forget it, I'll find another auto
phrases.context: The walk-away — most effective negotiation tool
What the driver says when he agrees — you won!

grammar_notes: 'na' at the end of any sentence softens it — 'chalo' (go!) vs 'chalo na' (please go)
'chaloge?' = will you go? — future tense with polite 'ge' ending
culture_notes: Always negotiate BEFORE sitting in the auto
Saying 'meter se chalo' is your right but most drivers refuse — so negotiate a fair price
Walking away is the strongest move — they'll often call you back

skill_breakdown.explanation: Structure: verb root + raha/rahe/rahi + hoon/hai/ho/hain. Gender matters: male = raha, female = rahi, plural/respectful = rahe.
Giving commands/requests. 'Tu' form: verb root (ja!). 'Tum' form: root + o (jao). 'Aap' form: root + iye (jaiye). Add 'na' to soften any of them.
Hindi has many ways to be firm but polite: 'na' (please), 'yaar' (dude/friend), 'bhaiya/didi' (brother/sister), 'zara' (just/a little). Stack them to sound natural.

more_examples.english: reduce it a bit please dude
brother, don't ask that much at least

practice_prompt: You're outside Saket metro station. You need to get to Select Citywalk mall (5 min drive). An auto driver says 150 rupees. You know it should be 40-50. Negotiate him down — use 'bhaiya', 'na', try the walk-away if needed.
